item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure if this is anabasis related or not.
But I read it all, no skimming, so +1

edit: found the crosslink, nice work

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just goose bumps at the end, sheesh, I really like this one. Good read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the corpse is constantly releasing bacteria, yet isn't decaying, doesn't it violate conservation of energy/mass? In which case the SCP should be tagged "ectoentropic"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

She mentions that she's killed every person she's ever touched. I think the prosthetics were meant to be an attempt at partial biocontainment so she could at least somewhat interact with people without infecting them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is what I was trying to suggest. The bacteria seem to me like they're just as anomalous as the woman herself, and I'd like to hear more about them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Errr… I think your tone here's a bit needlessly hostile. I'm pretty sure she was just suggesting that a few more details about the bacteria and how it does what it does as well as some better containment measures would improve the article - which I completely agree with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I think I like this a lot because this article has about the best, coldest scientific tone I think I've seen in a skip, and yet still keeps me interested and reading. Your tone is so cold and dead, and yet it still gives me the feels, even before I reached the letter.

I'm still not sure whats going on with the Prosthesis… like what her (assumed) goal was, but its got me interested and thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What, you wanted the entire biological cycle of the bacteria? You know that would just lead to more cries of 'wuh this isn't scientifically possible' because the oxygen-carbon rate is wrong or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very upvoted! : D (The written records addendum was sad.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to downvote because so much is vague around the aerobic bacteria aspect, how it spreads, and why there is no apparent use of hazmat suits or even just keeping the body in a negative-pressure chamber to prevent the bacteria from spreading to the rest of the site.

The letter is a piece of art and I love how the prosthetics make sense in light of it. I also really like whenever an SCP explicitly involves prosopagnosia, because it's something I have. These things make me want to upvote, but the article really needs more safety dance.

No-voting for now.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biosafety_level
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biocontainment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. I have nothing else to say really. The letter did it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was the letter that did it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is that an Emperor Norton reference I spy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure how to deal with the more pressing details you bring up (not yet, at least), but you make good points with the other ones. I wasn't clear in the description but I wanted to convey that they didn't move the body because it was stuck, and moving it would likely end up damaging the body, at which point it would probably just be easier to maintain the property around it rather than bring in heavy equipment to demolish the place and scoop it out with a mass of soil. So long as it's maintained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this one, and I commend the commitment to narrative that I like to see in articles. However, I feel that you get bogged down a little bit in the discovery portion of the article, and I wonder why the Foundation didn't just relocate this to some sort of hermetically-sealed chamber away from a population center when they found out what it's doing. I also am sort of confused as to what exactly this thing is. I mean, it's okay that I the reader don't know the answer, but I feel like I don't even know where to begin looking on this one.

Neutral vote. I think this article will end up succeeding, but can't upvote this as currently written.

Also, quick nitpick: "gentrification" is a term that is usually used by opponents of development as a negative characterization, so a Foundation front would be unlikely to acquire a property with the stated goal of "gentrifying" an area. Best, in my opinion, for the Foundation to come in and just eminent domain the property through contacts at the local government level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No. Otzi isn't cursed, =P that's just legend… or fodder for a separate SCP?!

I hadn't thought of things like Otzi though; Otzi's body is actually desiccated and mummified. My SCP is meant to look freshly dead, as in still with pinkish skin and squishy parts.

I was actually inspired more by the myths of the "incorruptibles" http://skeptoid.com/episodes/4126

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this resembles Ötzi on being a well-preserved human body, but the rest is really different.
And I just found that Ötzi is cursed, while SCP-1856 is corrosive. Is this another similarity?
